children.name: Cricetus
children.description: The common hamster — once one of the most abundant mammals of the European steppe and farmland, now critically endangered across western Europe. It is a solitary, aggressive rodent that hoards enormous quantities of seeds and roots in its burrow system — up to 65 kg have been found in a single cache. Common hamsters hibernate individually and have been recorded carrying grain in their pouches by running back to their burrows hundreds of times in a single day. Intensive agriculture, pesticide use, and the conversion of grain crops to maize (which provides poor nutrition) have caused catastrophic population collapses.

children.name: Cricetus cricetus
children.description: Critically endangered in France, Belgium, and the Netherlands. Larger than pet hamsters with striking black, white, and chestnut patterning. Will aggressively rear up and hiss at threats much larger than itself.

children.name: Mesocricetus
children.description: Golden hamsters — small solitary rodents native to the dry scrubland of Syria and Turkey. All golden hamsters in captivity worldwide descend from a single wild female and her pups captured near Aleppo, Syria in 1930. In the wild, golden hamsters are nocturnal, solitary, and highly aggressive towards conspecifics outside the breeding season. They were introduced to western laboratories as research animals in the 1930s and became popular pets in the 1940s. Wild populations are now threatened by habitat loss and are considered vulnerable.

children.name: Mesocricetus auratus
children.description: The familiar pet hamster. Wild populations survive in a small area of northwestern Syria and are listed as vulnerable. Their cheek pouches extend back along the sides of their body to their hips.

children.name: Microtus
children.description: Voles — short-tailed, small-eyed grassland and woodland rodents that are among the most abundant mammals on Earth. Vole populations undergo dramatic cycles, typically peaking every 3–5 years then crashing. These cycles drive corresponding cycles in owl, hawk, and fox populations across the Northern Hemisphere. The common vole can produce up to 7 litters of 4–8 young per year, reaching sexual maturity at just 2–3 weeks of age. They create dense runway systems through grass and are critical to the ecology of virtually every northern terrestrial ecosystem.

children.name: Microtus arvalis
children.description: The most important prey item for barn owls, kestrels, and rough-legged buzzards across Europe. Population explosions can strip cereal crops. Capable of producing 15–18 young in a single month.

children.name: Microtus agrestis
children.description: Britain's most abundant small mammal. Prefers longer, rougher grass than the common vole. A barn owl family needs roughly 1,000 field voles per year to successfully raise a brood.

children.name: Lemmus
children.description: True lemmings of the Arctic tundra — stout, furry rodents famous for dramatic population cycles. Every 3–5 years, lemming populations explode then crash, driving corresponding crashes in snowy owl, Arctic fox, and rough-legged buzzard populations. The myth of mass suicide — lemmings throwing themselves off cliffs — was popularised by a staged Disney documentary in 1958; in reality, lemmings migrate in search of food and some drown crossing rivers, but there is no suicidal behaviour. Lemmings are active year-round under the snow, where insulated subnivean runways maintain temperatures above freezing.

children.name: Lemmus lemmus
children.description: The most colourful rodent of northern Europe — boldly patterned in black, rust, and white. The national animal of Norwegian folklore. Snowy owl breeding success is almost entirely dependent on lemming population peaks.

children.name: Myodes
children.description: Bank voles — woodland-adapted voles with a redder coat and more prominent ears than field voles. The bank vole is one of the most widespread small mammals in European woodland and is particularly associated with dense, shrubby understorey. It is an important vector of hantavirus in Europe. Unlike field voles, bank voles are good climbers and will ascend into shrubs and low branches to eat berries and catkins. They are often the only small mammal present in dense conifer plantations.

children.name: Myodes glareolus
children.description: Distinguished from other voles by its chestnut back. A key prey species for tawny owls, weasels, and stoats in woodland. Puschkinia lemming, a hantavirus, is carried asymptomatically by bank voles in Scandinavia.

children.name: Ondatra
children.description: The muskrat — a large, semi-aquatic vole native to North America, now established across much of Europe and Asia following escapes from fur farms in the early 20th century. Despite its name and superficial appearance, the muskrat is not a true rat but a highly modified vole. It builds lodge-like structures from vegetation in marshes and ponds, and its burrows can undermine dykes and riverbanks. It is one of the most important fur-bearing animals in North America; tens of millions were trapped annually in the mid-20th century.

children.name: Ondatra zibethicus
children.description: A significant invasive species in Europe, where it is controlled to protect agricultural drainage infrastructure. Swims using a laterally flattened, scaly tail. Can stay submerged for up to 17 minutes.
